llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: After careful re-reading, this paper does not fit the SPEED framework. The study evaluates AI-designed tax policies (the 'AI Economist' uses reinforcement learning to learn optimal tax schedules) against baseline tax policy mechanisms (free market, US federal tax schedule, Saez formula). The comparisons are between different economic policy regimes, not between human workers performing tasks with versus without AI assistance. In the AI experiments (Section 4), AI agents play an economic simulation game under different tax policies. In the human experiments (Section 5), human participants play the same game under different tax policies. In both cases, the 'AI' refers to the mechanism that designed the tax policy, not a productivity tool assisting human workers. There is no human-only baseline arm in the sense required for SPEED: a condition where human workers perform tasks without AI assistance, compared to the same workers (or comparable workers) performing the same tasks with AI assistance. The 'free market' arm is a no-tax policy baseline, not a human-only work condition. The human experiments compare tax policy outcomes (productivity, equality) across four tax regimes (free market, US federal, Saez, AI Economist), but these are comparisons of policy mechanisms, not human labor productivity with/without AI assistance. The paper reports means for productivity (coins produced) under each tax policy, but these are outcomes of the tax policies themselves applied to either AI agents or human participants, not measures of human worker productivity with/without AI assistance. Sample sizes are reported as number of episodes or participants across all conditions, not per-arm in a way that allows extraction for a human vs. human+AI comparison. The paper does report that the AI Economist achieves 16% improvement in equality-productivity trade-off over Saez (Figure 8), but this is a composite metric comparing policy outcomes, not a human productivity comparison. No standard deviations, standard errors, t-statistics, or regression coefficients comparing human-only vs. human+AI productivity are reported in a way that fits the SPEED framework.
llm_extracted.notes: This paper fundamentally does not fit the SPEED framework. It is a study of AI-designed economic policies (tax schedules learned via reinforcement learning) compared to baseline tax policies, not a study of human worker productivity with versus without AI assistance. The 'AI' in 'AI Economist' refers to the mechanism that designed the tax policy, not a tool that assists human workers in performing tasks. The comparisons are between policy mechanisms (AI Economist vs. free market vs. US federal vs. Saez formula), not between human workers performing tasks with vs. without AI assistance. In the AI experiments, AI agents play an economic simulation game under different tax policies. In the human experiments, human participants play the same game under different tax policies. In both cases, the outcome (productivity = coins produced) is a measure of the tax policy's effect on economic outcomes, not a measure of human worker productivity with/without AI assistance. There is no human-only baseline arm in the sense required for SPEED. The 'free market' arm is a no-tax policy baseline, not a human-only work condition. The paper reports means for productivity under each tax policy (Figure 8: AI experiments - Free Market 3285, US Federal 2656, Saez 2907, AI Economist 2907; Figure 17: human experiments - Free Market 2605, US Federal 1658, Saez 1658, AI Economist 2339), but these are outcomes of the tax policies themselves, not measures of human worker productivity with/without AI assistance. Sample sizes are reported as number of episodes or participants across all conditions (Section 5.2: 'first batch consisted of 57 episodes with 58 participants', 'second batch consisted of 68 episodes with 57 participants'), not per-arm in a way that allows extraction for a human vs. human+AI comparison. The paper does report that the AI Economist achieves 16% improvement in equality-productivity trade-off over Saez (page 20: 'The AI Economist achieves a 16% gain improvement over the next best model, which is the Saez model'), but this is a composite metric (equality × productivity) comparing policy outcomes, not a human productivity comparison. No standard deviations, standard errors, t-statistics, or regression coefficients comparing human-only vs. human+AI productivity are reported in a way that fits the SPEED framework. The paper does report statistical tests for the human experiments (Section 5.2: 'two-sided t-test for the alternate hypothesis Ya;vw ≠ 0, with p = 0.05'), but these compare tax policy regimes, not human+AI vs. human-only productivity. After exhaustive re-reading, I confirm that this paper does not contain the data needed to compute a SPEED effect size.
source_quotes: We propose a two-level deep reinforcement learning approach to learn dynamic tax policies, based on economic simulations in which both agents and a government learn and adapt.
AI-driven tax policies improve the trade-off between equality and productivity by 16% over baseline policies, including the prominent Saez tax framework.

Productivity (left panel, higher is better) measures the total amount of income generated within an episode (analogous to GDP). Taxation always results in a decrease of productivity when compared with the free market, but the loss in productivity is the smallest under the AI Economist.

We ran all experiments with US-based participants on Amazon Mechanical Turk. Participants performed HITs (Human Intelligence Task). Each HIT consists of a sequence of four episodes, with a tutorial before each episode, and a post-episode survey.

In experiments with human participants, the 'Camelback' tax schedule achieves an equality-productivity trade-off that is comparable to the Saez model, and with better equality-productivity performance than the US Federal and free-market approaches.
Coin Produced: Free Market 3285, US Federal 2656, Saez Formula 2907, AI Economist 2907 (Figure 8, AI experiments).
Coin Produced: Free Market 2605, US Federal 1658, Saez Formula 1658, AI Economist 2339 (Figure 17, human experiments).
The AI Economist achieves a 16% gain improvement over the next best model, which is the Saez model (page 20).

For the first batch, we test whether the difference in the social welfare between the tax models is statistically significant. In particular, for each participant a, we compute the mean value Zai of the social objective (e.g., eq × prod) under each tax model i. We then perform a two-sided t-test for the alternate hypothesis Ya;vw ≠ 0, with p = 0.05. (Section 5.2)
